title: Giving & Generosity
description: In this lesson you will explore why generosity and tithing flows from gratitude, not obligation, and why giving is the clearest thermometer of where your heart actually is. Knowing that every thing is God's and you are His steward breaks the grip of money on our lives and will bring you lasting financial freedom.

blocks.text: In the last six lessons, you have explored what it looks like to love the people right in front of you. You have looked at your neighbors, your family, your church, your spiritual gifts, and the reality of suffering. You have stood in the beauty of worship as a way of life. If worship is declaring what God is worth, there may be no louder way to declare it than with your money. So now we turn to a very personal question: what does your money tell you about what you actually believe?
Exposing Your Heart

Jesus talked about money and possessions more than almost any other subject, more than heaven, more than hell, more than prayer. Sixteen of His thirty-eight parables deal with money or possessions. He seems to have understood something that most of us resist: money is one of the most spiritually exposing things we handle. It has the power to reveal what we really trust, what we really love, and what we think will ultimately keep us safe. And not only the money we give to the church, or to charity, but all of it, because every bit of our wealth is actually God's, and God is very clear on how we should view His money.

Despite what some believe, Jesus was not anti-wealth. Some of His most devoted followers were wealthy. But He was relentless about the heart posture that money tends to produce, the subtle shift from holding things open-handed to holding them so tightly that we forget they were never really ours to begin with. He diagnosed the problem this way:
No one can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.

Notice what Jesus did not say. He did not say you should not serve both. He did not say it is hard to serve both. He said you cannot. Money wants to be your master. It wants your trust when the bills pile up. It wants your attention first thing in the morning. It wants you to believe that your security comes from a number in your account instead of a God who owns everything. At some point, every person has to choose. Generosity is what happens when God wins that choice, when you say with your wallet what you say with your mouth: He is enough.
7.1 - What do you think it means "to serve" or to "be devoted to" money?
Everything Belongs to God
The foundation of biblical generosity is not primarily about percentages or budgets. It is about ownership. Who does your money actually belong to? Scripture has a clear answer: God. All of it.
The earth is the Lord's, and everything in it, the world, and all who live in it.

This is not a metaphor. God owns the earth. He owns your house, your car, your retirement account, and your salary. You are a manager, not an owner. Everything you have has been entrusted to you, not to keep and protect as your permanent possession but to manage wisely for His purposes during the time you have it. Think about how many things we "own" and in just a few short years there will be other people who say they "own" those things, and others decades from now will also claim them: homes, land, possessions, trinkets or treasures. Stewardship is managing what God owns for God's purposes during the time He has entrusted it to you.

When stewardship is your perspective, giving is no longer a sacrifice you make from "your money" but a return of what has always been His. The question is not, How much of my money should I give to God? The question is, How much of God's money should I keep? That is stewardship thinking. And it is radically freeing.
7.2 - If you are a manager of God's money and not the owner, how does this change the way you think about money?
Example of the Widow

Jesus was in the temple, watching people drop in their offerings. The wealthy gave large amounts that were impressive to many. Then a poor widow came and put in two small copper coins, worth a fraction of a penny.
Jesus stopped everything and called His disciples over.

Truly, I say to you, this poor widow has put in more than all those who are contributing to the offering box. For they all contributed out of their abundance, but she out of her poverty has put in everything she had, all she had to live on.

The point is not the amount. It is the heart, and equally, the trust. The wealthy gave from their excess, and she gave from her poverty. She had decided that God was more reliable than the two coins she was holding onto. That is not foolishness. That is faith you can see with your eyes. And it is worship, declaring with everything she had that God was worth more than her last meal.
Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.

The word cheerful in Greek is hilaros, it is the root of our word hilarious. God loves a hilarious giver. Not a guilty giver. Not a peer-pressured giver. Not a calculated giver who gives the minimum required. A giver who gives so freely and so joyfully that it looks a little crazy from the outside. Because it is. That is the nature of genuine trust.
Examine Why You Give

a) The one who gives like paying taxes. She gives because she has to.

b) The one who gives like paying an electric bill. She gives because she ought to.

c) The one who gives like buying a present for someone she loves, like the widow. She gives because she wants to and she cannot help it.

How your heart responds to generous living will either reveal the private idol worship of money very quickly, or show you a hilarious and joyful giver.

7.3 - When you give financially, to your church, to missions, to people in need, does it feel more like you are paying your taxes, paying for a service, or buying a present for someone you care about? Explain your answer.
Establish Your Giving Plan
Most Christians begin their financial journey giving little or nothing. The path to genuine, joy-filled generosity grows through three stages.

Level 1: Begin Generosity
Start somewhere. Create the habit of giving first, taking an amount off the top of every paycheck before spending anything else. Many begin at 1–2% and grow from there. This step begins transforming your heart toward a right view of money.

Level 2: Faithful Generosity
Move to planned, intentional giving. Christians have long started with the tithe of 10%. Direct your gifts purposefully to your local church, missions, the poor, and needs God places before you.

Level 3: Eternal Generosity
Adopt what Pastor John Piper calls a “wartime lifestyle.” Consider a capped lifestyle so when extra resources do arrive, you joyfully fuel urgent Kingdom work, reaching the unreached and advancing the gospel. This proves God provides far more peace and security than money.
7.4 - What is your plan for giving?

When you embrace generosity as joyful stewardship, money loses its grip on your heart and you step into freedom. You move from anxiously holding onto what was never truly yours to cheerfully investing in what lasts forever: transformed lives, the spread of the gospel, and the glory of God. There is deep, hilarious joy waiting for every believer who learns to give freely, because in generosity we discover that God really is enough. That is worship and Eternal Generosity.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
